user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: inc/web_assets.h
## Active selection of the file:
web_assets_list
## My request for Codex:
In `lib\WifiManager\WebServer.cpp` abd `lib\WifiManager\WebServer.h`, use `ESPAsyncWebServer`, have a initialization function that registers all server request handlers

the init function should iterate through all items in the list `web_assets_list` and use the same handler for each item. The handler itself will check the GET request parameters again to see which payload is to be served.
use a streaming reply method if we are worried about file sizes being too big for RAM.
the contents shouldn't need to be decompressed when sent, use the reply headers to notify the browser that the reply is compressed
agent_reply: - Registers one exact GET route per asset using the same handler.
- Adds `/` as an alias for `index.html` when it exists.
The shared handler looks up the requested URL again, finds the matching `web_asset_desc_t`, and sends the already-gzipped payload with:

It serves the compressed byte array directly with the library’s byte-array response path, so it does not decompress or build a giant RAM buffer first. The upstream library describes `ESPAsyncWebServer` as an asynchronous HTTP server with exact/prefix/regex routing support, which matches this registration approach. ([github.com](https://github.com/ESP32Async/ESPAsyncWebServer/blob/main/README.md))
One small integration note: `WebServer::init()` exists now, but I did not call it from Wi-Fi activation yet.

commit_message: starting to add a web server

agent_reply: Done. `WebServer.cpp` is now mostly auth, asset serving, routing, and FTP startup. user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/Security/Aegis.h
## Active selection of the file:
setTestTempPassword
## My request for Codex:
we are reworking how security works, there are now two keys, filecrypt-key and network-key, I want the filecrypt key to have limited exposure.
first, fill in `kSaltNetwork` with "36 22 5c 86 c1 70 f2 c1 11 d3 d6 df 57 6e 28 93 71 4e 52 6c d8 43 b2 6b df ee 1f 12 c9 a3 e5 b2 "

then extend `Aegis::init` to load two keys into memory, `hasMasterKey` should not be renamed and reflect if both keys are available. But `getMasterKey` and `setMasterKey` and similar getters and setters now need to reflect the names `filecrypt` and `network`
`setTestTempMasterKey` also needs to be split into two and `setTestTempPassword` needs to create both keys
agent_reply: - `Aegis::init()` now loads both `filecrypt_key` and `network_key` from NVS.
- `hasMasterKey()` still exists, and now returns true only when both keys are available.
- `setTestTempPassword()` now derives both keys using separate salts.
- Audio recording encryption now uses only the filecrypt key.
- Web auth/config encryption/cloud upload auth now use the network key.
- Updated `test_sdcard()` to install deterministic test keys for both slots.
Also made `Aegis::init()` open NVS read-only and close the handle after loading; setters open write access only when actually saving keys.
Build passed. I restored `src/version.c` after PlatformIO regenerated it.
- `Aegis::init()` now loads both `filecrypt_key` and `network_key` from NVS.
- `hasMasterKey()` still exists, and now returns true only when both keys are available.
- `setTestTempPassword()` now derives both keys using separate salts.
- Audio recording encryption now uses only the filecrypt key.
- Web auth/config encryption/cloud upload auth now use the network key.
- Updated `test_sdcard()` to install deterministic test keys for both slots.
Also made `Aegis::init()` open NVS read-only and close the handle after loading; setters open write access only when actually saving keys.
Build passed. I restored `src/version.c` after PlatformIO regenerated it.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h
## Active selection of the file:
WebServer
## My request for Codex:
we need a session-security-state type of structure to hold web browser session security information
only one session is allowed, even having two browser windows open is not allowed and will simply cause stuff to fail
the handler for "/get_info" needs to
* randomly generate 32 bytes, called the session-challenge, and remember this
* using the network-key and session-challenge, perform hmacSha256, derive a session-response-from-client, and remember this
* using the network-key and session-response-from-client, perform hmacSha256, derive a session-response-from-server, and remember this
* randomly generate 16 bytes, called the session-salt-from-server, and remember this
* the response from "/get_info" needs to include: session-challenge, session-response-from-server, and session-salt-from-server
* internal nonce counter is reset to 0
on the client (javascript), a similar object needs to track the session security information
the javascript ajax call into "/get_info" needs to:
* randomly generate 16 bytes, called session-salt-from-client, remember this, during the call into "/get_info", this is included
upon receiving a reply from "/get_info", which includes session-challenge, session-response-from-server, and session-salt-from-server, all of this needs to be remembered
at this point, the user has not entered in a password, we do not have the key to do anything, we cannot generate any of the responses
at some point, the user will enter in a password. When this happens:
* derive the network-key from the password (a copy of `kSaltNetwork` needs to be included in the javascript file)
* derive the session-response-from-client (from the session-challenge and network-key)
* derive the session-response-from-server (from session-response-from-client and network-key)
* validate the computed-session-response-from-server against the received-session-response-from-server
* if invalid, then the password is wrong or the server is malfunctioning
* if valid, continue
* generate 16 bytes randomly as session-salt-from-client
* session-salt is the 32 byte concatenation of session-salt-from-server and session-salt-from-client
* derive session-key from the network-key and session-salt
* use ajax to request "/get_cfg", included in the request header are: session-response-from-client, session-salt-from-client
* internal nonce counter is reset to 0
* delete the entire `#login_panel` from the page, and blank out any variables that may contain the password. All keys are to be stored securely using WebCrypto if possible
The C++ backend needs to have "/get_cfg" modified to handle all of that
* validate session-response-from-client using network-key and session-challenge
* return error code if not validated
* if validated continue
* session-salt is the 32 byte concatenation of session-salt-from-server and session-salt-from-client
* compute the session-key with the network-key and session-salt
* construct the reply with the information as JSON but as a gigantic string
* encrypt the gigantic string using session-key
* send the encrypted reply
When we get the data from "/get_cfg"
* decrypt the data into JSON
* if not authentic, stop and warn the user.
* if authentic, continue
* fill the page elements with data from the JSON
delete `WebServer::authenticateRequest` and all usages of it, later when decryption is implemented, the decryption results will indicate if the request is authentic.
do tasks up to here so I have a chance to review, I'll issue another prompt for "/set_cfg", which would reuse a ton of what has been built up to this point
agent_reply: Implemented the session-security pass up through `/get_cfg`.
- Added single-session state and nonce helpers in [WebServer.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h:23).

- `/get_info` now creates the challenge, server salt, server response, resets nonce state, and returns those fields in JSON from [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:263).
- Replaced the old timestamp/hash auth with session-response validation in [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:453).

- `/get_cfg` now validates the client response, derives a session key, builds one JSON string, AES-GCM encrypts it as one blob, and returns `magic4|version1|ciphertext|tag16` from [WebCfgHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebCfgHandlers.cpp:1015).
- Removed all `WebServer::authenticateRequest` usages, including file upload and set_cfg request-level auth.

- Frontend now derives the network key, validates server response, derives/imports the session AES key, requests `/get_cfg`, decrypts it, and fills page fields in [script.js](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:797).
- Updated generated [web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h) from the JS changes.

One important caveat: browser login depends on `crypto.subtle`. If the browser refuses WebCrypto on the device’s plain HTTP origin, the page will alert and stop at login. Also, when we do `/set_cfg`, we should give client-to-server encryption a separate nonce domain or derived subkey so AES-GCM never reuses nonce `0` with the same session key.
Implemented the session-security pass up through `/get_cfg`.
- Added single-session state and nonce helpers in [WebServer.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h:23).

- `/get_info` now creates the challenge, server salt, server response, resets nonce state, and returns those fields in JSON from [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:263).
- Replaced the old timestamp/hash auth with session-response validation in [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:453).

- `/get_cfg` now validates the client response, derives a session key, builds one JSON string, AES-GCM encrypts it as one blob, and returns `magic4|version1|ciphertext|tag16` from [WebCfgHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebCfgHandlers.cpp:1015).
- Removed all `WebServer::authenticateRequest` usages, including file upload and set_cfg request-level auth.

- Frontend now derives the network key, validates server response, derives/imports the session AES key, requests `/get_cfg`, decrypts it, and fills page fields in [script.js](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:797).
- Updated generated [web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h) from the JS changes.

One important caveat: browser login depends on `crypto.subtle`. If the browser refuses WebCrypto on the device’s plain HTTP origin, the page will alert and stop at login. Also, when we do `/set_cfg`, we should give client-to-server encryption a separate nonce domain or derived subkey so AES-GCM never reuses nonce `0` with the same session key.

commit_message: I'm checking in this state before tearing it all down

user_prompt: no, only encrypt the file name with the session key, no other parameters, I see no reason why this wouldn't work in the backend if the backend has a full copy of the session key as well
agent_reply: Done. The decrypted download URL now carries only the encrypted filename:
No session response, no session salt, no JSON/action wrapper.
- Added `WebServer::copyCachedSessionKey()` so the GET handler can use the cached session key.
- `GET /decrypted_download` now decrypts only the `file_name` parameter with that cached session key.
- It validates the decrypted name as a `.rec` path, then streams it as an attachment.
- Encrypts just the raw filename string with the session AES key.
- Navigates to the GET URL so the browser treats it like a real download.
Done. The decrypted download URL now carries only the encrypted filename:
No session response, no session salt, no JSON/action wrapper.
- Added `WebServer::copyCachedSessionKey()` so the GET handler can use the cached session key.
- `GET /decrypted_download` now decrypts only the `file_name` parameter with that cached session key.
- It validates the decrypted name as a `.rec` path, then streams it as an attachment.
- Encrypts just the raw filename string with the session AES key.
- Navigates to the GET URL so the browser treats it like a real download.
